opportunities.title: Another Bridge, Another Flash Loan: Allbridge's $1.65M Was Never Going to Be the Last
opportunities.why_now: Allbridge Core just paused after a $1.65M flash loan exploit, the latest in a bridge-hack pattern that's been priced in for years
opportunities.angle: Security post-mortem: walk through the exploit mechanics, then zoom out to a running tally of bridge hacks in 2026 and why 'cross-chain' still means 'cross-your-fingers'

opportunities.title: Institutions Are Finally Admitting Audits Don't Save You — We Could Have Told Them in 2022
opportunities.why_now: Hacken's new report shows institutional trust in audits is cracking, validating years of 'audited' protocols that still got drained
opportunities.angle: Contrarian explainer: list high-profile hacks that happened post-audit, argue audits are a compliance checkbox not a security guarantee, position what actually matters (monitoring, incident response, insurance)

opportunities.title: SummerFi Is Dead. Seven Years of Trust, Killed by One Exploit
opportunities.why_now: SummerFi announced it's winding down after seven years, explicitly citing an exploit as the reason — a clean case study in how a single incident erases a protocol's entire track record
opportunities.angle: Obituary/lesson format: what SummerFi did right for years, what the exploit was, and why 'time in market' means nothing once a hack lands — reputational death is instant

opportunities.title: Consensys Hired a North Korean Developer and Didn't Know It — This Is the New Attack Surface
opportunities.why_now: Consensys confirmed it unknowingly outsourced dev work to a DPRK operative, showing that supply-chain infiltration via hiring is now a mainstream crypto security threat
opportunities.angle: Threat-model breakdown: how DPRK's fake-developer playbook works (stolen IDs, remote contracts, laptop farms), why it's spreading beyond exchanges into core infra teams, and what hiring managers should actually check

opportunities.title: Upbit's Parent Faces Sanctions 8 Months After Its $30M Hack — The Bill Always Comes Due, Just Late
opportunities.why_now: South Korean regulators are only now moving on Dunamu, nearly eight months after the Upbit hack, underscoring how slow regulatory consequences trail security failures
opportunities.angle: Timeline piece: hack date vs. sanction date, mapped against every other major exchange hack's regulatory lag, making the case that 'no immediate penalty' never means 'no penalty'
